CalifOrnia
Fair Political
Practices Cornrnission
February 6, ]-99L
Daniel R. Hume
1722 Mitchel1, #181
Tustin, cA 926ao
Re: Your Request for Advice
our FiIe No. A-91-038
Dear Mr. Hume:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
j.ng your responsibil ities under the conf I ict-of- interest provi-
sions of the PoLitical Reform Act (the trActr1-r7
OUESTTON
what are your disclosure and disqual ification obligations
with respect to sources of income situated outside your
j ur isd iction?
CONCLUS ION
If your clients are outside the jurisdiction 
and do not
conduct Lusiness within the jurisdiction, 
are not planning to do
business within the jurisdiction, 
and have not done business
within the jurisdiction during the two years prior.to the tine any
statenent oi other action is required under this tit1e, they are
not considered sources of income with respect to the disclosure or
disqual ification requirements of the Act.
FACTS
You are a real estate appraiser with the orange county
Assessor's office. 
You are considering working as a tax
consultant in your spare tirne. You anticipate pursuing-this
practJ-ce outside orange County and do not befieve you will be
l/ 
Government code Sections 8l-ooo-9101-5. A11 statutory
references are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated.
commission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
Section 18OOO; et seq. elf references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulatj'ons'
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O? . sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 o (916) 322'5660


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-038
Page 2
recej.ving income from any individuals or companies that do
business in orange County. You have asked whether such a business
will result in conflicts of interest lrith respect to your official
duti,es.
ANALYSTS
The Polj,tical Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
State of Cali.fornia by initiatj.ve in 1974. The Purpose for the
discLosure and disqual if ication provisions of the Act was to
ensure that public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would
perform their duties in an irnpartial lDanner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
pErsons who have supported thern. (Section 8r-001(b).)
Thus, under the Act, every public official 
rnust disclose all
his or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected
by the exercise of the official's 
duties. 
(Sections 81002(c),
8?2oo-8?313.) Further, section 87100 of the Act prohibits any
public official 
from makingf, participating in naking, or otherwise
using his or her official 
position to influence a governmental
deciiion in which the official 
has a financial interest.
A rrpublic official'r 
is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulati-on 18700 (coPy enclosed) as every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or locaI
government agency. As a real estate appraiser with the orange
6ounty Assessor'i office it appears you are a rrpublic officialrr 
as
defined in the Act.
Econornic Interests
section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision witl have a rnaterial financial effect, distinguishable
frorn the effect on the public generally, on the off i-cia1 or a
member of his or her irnmediate farnily or on any econornic interest
including:
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercj-al lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public lrithout regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or pronised to the public official
wi.thin 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is rnade.
section 87■03(c)


Our Fi■e NO. A-9■-038
Page 3
Ho$/ever, section 82030 (a) provides:
rrlncomett means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), a payment received, including but
not linited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend,
interest, rent, proceeds from any sa1e, gift,
including any gift of food or beverage, 1oan,
forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by
the fi1er, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or
contribution to an insurance or pension program
paid by any person other than an employer, and
including any community property interest in the
income of a spouse. Income also includes an
outstanding Ioan. Income of an individual also
includes a pro rata share of any incorne of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, dj,rectly, indirectly or beneficially,
a 1o-percent interest or greater. 
rrfncome, rr other
than a oift. 
does not include incone received from
done business within the iurisdiction durinq the
two years prior to the time anv statement or other
action is reouired under this title.
(Emphasis added. )
Thus, if your cIj-ents are outsj,de the jurisdiction and do not
conduct business within the iurisdiction, 
are not olanninq to do
business within the jurisdiction, 
and have not done busj-ness
wittrin ttre jurisdiction during the two years prJ'or to the time any
statement or other action is reguired under this titIe, 
they are
not considered sources of income.
In L97g the Commission examined the criteria used to
deterrnine hrhether an investment interest vras rrdoing businessrr in
an official's 
iurisdiction in In re Baty (5 FPPC ops. 10, copy
enclosedl .2/ in that opinion, the conmission stated:
If an entity in which an official- has an
investrnent has no business contacts with the
2/ see also, In re carev (t977) 3 FPPC oPS' 99 (copy.enclosed) ,
which concluaes ttrat ownersrrip or property in the jurisdiction
=i[iifi"= the "day businessrt ltandlrd. Section 82035 provides
inai rear property located within 2 rniles of the boundaries of the
j"ii.a:."ti'"r, ^o. ,itt itt 2 rniles of any land owned or used by the
iocal government agency shaIl be deemed to be within the
jurisdiction.


Our File No. A-9■
-038
Page 4
jurisdiction over which the officiaL's authority
extends, there is litt1e need for dj-sclosure of the
official's 
j-nvestment in the entity. 
Because of
the entity's l-ack of contacts with the
jurisdiction, 
the official 
is not 1ike1y to rnake
any decisions in his official 
capacity which have
the potentiaL for benefiting or injuring the
entity. 
Holrever, where the entity in which the
official 
has an investment has business contacts
with the official's 
jurisdiction, 
there is a
potential for conflict of interests arising and
substantial justification 
for disclosure of the
of f icial's 
investment.
***
Although none of the corporations is
headquartered in california, each has either
distribution or manufacturing facilities 
in
california and the products manufactured by each
are sold on a regular basis throughout the state.
Any of these activities 
engaged in by the
corporations standing alone would be sufficient to
create a potenti,al for conflicts of interest on the
part of an official 
having an investnent in the
corporations.
You stated that you anticipate your clients will be frorn
outside your jurisdiction and will not do business in Orange
County, If this is the case, you will not have disclosure or
disqua l ification responsibi I ities with respect to those sources of
incorne. However, where in fact a client does business in orange
county or Dlans to do so, the client is a source of incone under
the Act and you will have both disqual ification and disclosure
iespons irif ities wittr respect to that person.3/
rl 
cenerally, officials are presumed to know who have been
sources of income to them. An official 
knoi'/s or has reason to
know that a decision will affect a source of income whenever a
reasonable person, under the sarne circurnstances, would be likely
to kno!, the identity of the source of incorne and would be aware of
the decision's probable inpact on the source. (Price Advice
Letter, No. A-85-165, copy enclosed.) of course, whether you know
or have reason to know a-lerson is a source of rrincome" as defined
under the Act is necessarily a factual guestion and nust be
deterrnj-ned on a case-by-case basis.


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-038
Page 5
I hope these genera■
 gu■de■ines have addressed your concerns.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter′
 p■ease
feel free to contact me at (9■
6)322-590■
.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
